mechanism_narrative: The federal funds rate at 0.18% signals an exceptionally accommodative monetary stance, compressing discount rates and supporting equity valuations, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns over 20 trading days as long as rates remain near zero.
failure_condition: Walk-forward Rank IC over any 2-year rolling window falls below 0.03, or 20-day returns in periods with FEDFUNDS <= 0.25% are not significantly positive (one-sided t-test p>=0.05).

mechanism_narrative: A sustained decline in the unemployment rate from its cyclical peak signals labor market healing, which boosts consumer spending and corporate earnings, producing positive forward returns over a 60-day horizon when UNRATE drops meaningfully.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE declines by at least 0.3 percentage points month-over-month
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE at 9.4% in May 2009, near peak
failure_condition: In triggered instances, the mean 60-day forward return is not significantly positive (t-test p>=0.05) or the hit rate (positive returns) is below 55%.

mechanism_narrative: Near-zero policy rates combined with rising housing starts signal that monetary stimulus is gaining traction in the interest-sensitive housing sector, indicating a durable demand recovery that drives S&P 500 upward over 20 trading days.
failure_condition: When both conditions hold, the proportion of positive 20-day returns is below 60% or the mean return is indistinguishable from zero (t-test p>0.10).

mechanism_narrative: In a high-unemployment environment (UNRATE > 8%), a recovery in retail and food services sales (RSAFS) is an especially powerful positive signal for future equity returns because it directly confirms that the consumer sector is healing from crisis-induced weakness.
evidence_refs: fact:RSAFS at 339993M in May 2009, showing stabilization
failure_condition: Over all triggered instances with UNRATE > 8%, the mean 20-day forward return is not significantly positive (one-sided t-test p>=0.05).

mechanism_narrative: A sustained increase in industrial production (INDPRO) after a deep contraction signals that manufacturing activity is resuming, which leads to higher corporate profits and positive S&P 500 returns over a 60-day horizon as the supply side recovers.
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO at 97.07 in April 2009, near cyclical low
failure_condition: On triggered months, the mean 60-day forward return is negative or less than +1% with high variance, or the rank information coefficient is below 0.02 over a rolling 3-year window.

mechanism_narrative: Rising nonfarm payroll employment (PAYEMS) directly indicates improving labor demand and aggregate income, which supports consumer spending and sustains the economic recovery, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns over 60 trading days regardless of the wider regime.
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS at 132,151k in May 2009, still declining but decelerating
failure_condition: The rolling 3-year correlation between month-over-month PAYEMS change and forward 60-day SPX returns is below 0.10 or the mean returned excess return over risk-free is zero or negative.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) rebounding above the 500,000 level signals a turn in the housing market from crisis lows, which quickly improves sentiment and leads to short-term positive S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days as markets price in construction activity recovery.
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST at 458k in April 2009, approaching potential threshold
failure_condition: The average 5-day forward return after HOUST crosses 500 is negative or the hit rate is below 50% over the walk-forward period.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate is at the zero lower bound and CPI inflation shows month-over-month acceleration (indicating reflation), the combination of extremely accommodative policy and diminishing deflation risk strongly boosts equity prices over a 20-day horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:CPIAUCSL at 212.671 in April 2009, flat to slightly positive
regime_hint: zero lower bound with disinflation risk
failure_condition: In all instances where both conditions hold, the proportion of positive 20-day returns is below 55% or the mean return is not statistically greater than the sample average of all 20-day returns during the crisis period (t-test p>0.05).
